cards.text: A {{c1::dangling}} string_view occurs when the underlying string is destroyed but the string_view still references it.
Returning a std::string_view to a {{c1::local std::string}} creates a dangling string_view.
To extract a substring from string_view, use the {{c1::.substr()}} method: {{c2::sv.substr(pos, len)}}
To remove prefix/suffix from string_view, use {{c1::.remove_prefix(n)}} or {{c2::.remove_suffix(n)}}.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Undefined behavior: dangling string_view. The local string temp is destroyed when the function returns, but sv still references its memory.
cards.explanation: Never return a string_view to a local string. The string_view will outlive the string it references.

cards.question: Is this code safe?
cards.answer: No, undefined behavior. The string s is destroyed at the end of the block, leaving sv dangling.
cards.explanation: string_view doesn't own the string data, so it becomes invalid when the source string is destroyed.

cards.front: What is the key danger of std::string_view?
cards.back: Dangling views: if the underlying string is destroyed or goes out of scope, the string_view becomes invalid and accessing it causes undefined behavior.

cards.front: Why is it generally unsafe to store a std::string_view as a member variable?
cards.back: The string_view might outlive the string it references, creating a dangling view. The object would contain an invalid reference.

cards.explanation:
To use string_view safely:
  • Use primarily for function parameters
  • Ensure the source string outlives the string_view
  • Don't return string_view to local strings
  • Don't store as member variables (usually)
  • OK with string literals (they have static lifetime)
